content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
428 ,I Street• Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Eileen Monaghan Teichert 
SBCT A General Counsel 
1170 W. 3rd Street, 2nd Floor 
San Bernardino, CA 92410-1715 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-17-118 
Dear Ms. Teichert: 
June 16, 2017 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of San Bernardino County 
Transportation Authority Board of Directors Member Darcy McNaboe regarding the conflict of 
interest provisions of the Political Refonn Act (the "Act") 1 and Section 1090. Please note that we 
do not advise on any other area oflaw, including Public Contract Code or common law conflicts of 
interest. Please also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby 
( 1975) l FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. 
In regard to our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all 
pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Santa Bernardino 
County District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.l(c)(3).) We did not receive 
a written response from either entity. (Section 1097. l(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you 
that, for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding 
against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.l (c)(5).) 
QUESTIONS 
1. Does Section I 090 prohibit San Bernardino County Transportation Authority ("SBCTA") 
Board Member Darcy McNaboe from making or participating in making, or the SBCT A Board of 
Directors from entering into, an engineering services contract with Falcon Engineering Services, 
Inc. ('"Falcon Engineering") given that the Board Member's consulting finn provides Falcon 
Engineering with public-outreach services on construction projects that do not involve the SBCTA? 
2. Would Section 1090 prohibit the SBCT A Board of Directors from entering into the 
engineering services contract with Falcon Engineering if the Board Member and her consufting finn 
terminate its existing contracts and subcontracts for the provision of public-outreach services on 
behalf of Falcon Engineering? 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 8 I 000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No.A-17-118 
Page No. 2 
3. Would the Act prohibit the Board Member from taking part in governmental decisions 
relating to the engineering services contract with Falcon Engineering if the Board Member and her 
consulting firm terminate its existing contracts and subcontracts for the provision of public-outreach 
services on behalf of Falcon Engineering? 
CONCLUSIONS 
I. Yes. Section 1090 prohibits the Board Member from making or participating in the 
making of, and the SBCT A Board of Directors from entering into, the engineering services contract 
with Falcon Engineering. 
2. No. Section 1090 would not prohibit the SBCTA Board of Directors from entering into 
the engineering services contract with Falcon Engineering if the Board Member and her consulting 
firm terminate its existing contracts and subcontracts for the provision of public-outreach services 
on behalf of Falcon Engineering in a manner that removes the possibility of Falcon Engineering 
having any direct or indirect influence on the Board Member. 
3. The Act would prohibit the Board Member from taking part in decisions relating to the 
engineering services contract with Falcon Engineering for 12 months following the Board Member 
or her consulting firm's final receipt of income from Falcon Engineering. 
FACTS 
You are the SBCTA's General Counsel and the authorized representative ofSBCTA Board 
of Directors Member Darcy McNaboe. The SBCT A is a county-wide government transportation 
agency created pursuant to Chapter 7 (commencing with Section 130800) of Division 12 of the 
Public Utilities Code. The SBCT A has a twenty-nine-member Board of Directors consisting of the 
five San Bernardino County Supervisors and an elected official (a mayor or city councilmember) 
from each of the twenty-four incorporated cities in the County. 
The SBCT A is responsible for funding, studying, designing, constructing, and delivering 
various transportation projects throughout San Bernardino County, including highway widening, 
freeway interchanges, grade separations, and railway improvements, among other projects. The 
SBCT A has a small staff and contracts out almost all of the engineering services necessary for these 
projects. 
The SBCT A procures engineering services for its projects under a qualifications-based 
selection process, as required for state-funded projects by Chapter 10 (commencing with Section 
4525) of Division 5 of Title 1, and for federally funded projects by 40 United States Code Section 
1101 et seq. This process requires the SBCTA to form a contractor selection committee to rank the 
engineering firms that submit proposals in response to the RFP based primarily on those firms' 
qualifications and experience, and the SBCT A will then negotiate fair and reasonable compensation 
for the scope of activities described in the RFP with the highest ranked firm. SBCT A Board 
Members do not serve on the SBCT A's contractor selection panels and do not otherwise participate 
in the selection of engineering services contractors. However, the affirmative vote of a majority of 
SBCT A Board Members is required to award SBCT A engineering services contracts. 


File No. A-17-118 
Page No. 3 
The Board Member is also the Mayor of the City of Grand Terrace, one of the 24 cities 
within the County. The Board Member was appointed to the SBCTA Board in January of 2015. The 
Board Member is also the managing member of a public outreach consulting firm, Blue Mountain 
Village Enterprises LLC, doing business as Village Enterprises. Since approximately April of 2016, 
Village Enterprises either directly contracts with Falcon Engineering or subcontracts with a Falcon 
Engineering contractor, Green Com, Inc., to provide public outreach services for various Falcon 
Engineering construction projects that do not involve the SBCT A. The Board Member and Village 
Enterprises are financially compensated for its provision of public-outreach services on behalf of 
Falcon Engineering. 
The SBCTA will be releasing a RFP for engineering services for the SBCTA's U.S. 395 
widening project in the near future. Falcon Engineering may desire to submit a proposal in response 
to this RFP, and may desire to submit proposals in response to other SBCT A RFPs for engineering 
services in the future. 
ANALYSIS 
J. Does Section 1090 prohibit the Board Member from making or participating in the 
maldng of, or the SBCTA Board o(Directors from entering into. the engineering services contract 
with Falcon Engineering? 
Section I 090 prohibits a public official from being financially interested in a contract in both the 
official's public and private capacities. (Lexin v. Superior Court (20 I 0) 47Cal.4th 1050, 1073.) A 
contract that violates Section 1090 is void, regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair 
and equitable to all parties. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646-649.) When Section 1090 
is applicable to one member of a governing body of a public entity, the prohibition cannot be 
avoided by having the interested board member abstain; the entire governing body is precluded 
from entering into the contract. (Id. at pp. 647-649.) 
We employ a six-step analysis to determine whether an official has a disqualifying conflict 
of interest under Section I 090. These steps are: (I) does the matter involve a public officer or 
employee; (2) does the matter involve a contract; (3) is the official making or participating in 
making a contract; ( 4) does the official have a financial interest in the contract; ( 5) does a remote or 
noninterest exception apply; and, under appropriate facts, ( 6) does the rule of necessity apply? 
Steps one through four are not at issue here. A public officer is involved because the Board 
Member is a public officer due to her seryice on the SBCTA's Board of Directors and as the May,or 
of the City of Grand Terrace. A contract is involved because the SBCT A will contract with the 
engineering firm that submits the top-ranked proposal in response to the RFP. (See Diaz Advice 
Letter, No. A-16-214; Fowler Advice Letter, No. A-15-228; Chadwick Advice Letter, No. A 15-
147.) Because an officer is presumed to participate in the making of his or her agency's contracts 
when the officer is a member of a board or commission that has the authority to execute the contract 
at issue under Section 1090 (Thomson, supra, p. 649), the Board Member will be presumed to have 
participated in the making of the RFP and the resultant engineering services contract. 


File No. A-17-118 
Page No. 4 
A public officer who is employed by a contract provider of services to the officer's agency 
is financially interested in the agency's contracts with that contract provider. (58 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 
670 ( 1975); Diaz Advice Letter, supra; Scully Advice Letter, No. A-16-086; Rosenberg Advice 
Letter, No. 1-14-024). Accordingly, the Board Member is financially interested in the engineering 
services contract between the SBCT A and Falcon Engineering because Falcon Engineering is a 
source of income to the Board Member through Village Enterprises' existing contracts and 
subcontracts to provide public-outreach services for Falcon Engineering. 
Therefore, Section 1090 would prohibit the Board Member from making or participating in 
making, and the SBCTA from entering into, the engineering services contract with Falcon 
Engineering unless a remote or noninterest exception or the rule of necessity applies. 
Steps Five: Does a remote or noninterest exception apply? 
The Legislature has created various statutory exceptions to Section 1090's prohibition where 
the interest involved is deemed a "remote interest," as defined in Section 1091, or a "noninterest," 
as defined in Section 1091.5. However, none of these exceptions would apply to the facts presented. 
Step Six: Does the rule of necessity apply? 
In order to ensure that essential government functions are perfonned, the "rule of necessity" 
has been applied to permit a public officer to carry out an essential duty of his or her office despite 
a conflict of interest under Section 1090, where the officer, or the governing body of which the 
officer is a member, is the only one authorized to carry out that duty. (See 89 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 
217 (2006); 69 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen 102, 109 (1986); 65 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 305 ( 1982).) The rule of 
necessity is not applicable based on the facts presented, as another engineering finn besides Falcon 
Engineering could perfonn the engineering services contract. 
Thus, we conclude that Section 1090 would prohibit the Board Member from making or 
participating in making, and the SBCT A from entering into, the engineering services contract at 
issue with Falcon Engineering. 
2. Does Section 1090 prohibit the SBCTA Board o{Directors from entering into the 
engineering services contract with Falcon Engineering i{the Board Member and her consulting 
firm terminate its existing contracts and subcontracts for the provision o{public-outreach services 
on behal[o{Falcon Engineering? 
In 86 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 187 (2003), the Attorney General's Office determined that Section 
1090 does not include a "reach-back period." The opinion concluded that only during the pendency 
of a business relationship was there a financial interest from which the official might benefit 
directly or indirectly. However, if the business relationship is not terminated in a manner that 
removes "the possibility of any personal influence, either directly or indirectly," Section 1090's 
prohibition would remain in effect. (See, e.g., 89 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 69 (2006).) 
Therefore, so long as the Board Member and Village Enterprises tenninates its existing 
contracts and subcontracts for the provision of public-outreach services on behalf of Falcon 


File No. A-17-118 
Page No. 5 
Engineering in a manner that removes the possibility of Falcon Engineering having any direct or 
indirect influence on the Board Member, Section 1090 would not prohibit the SBCT A Board of 
Directors from entering into the engineering services contract with Falcon Engineering. 
3. Does the Act prohibit the Board Member fi·om taking part in governmental decisions 
relating to the engineering sen1ices contract with Falcon Engineering i(the Board Member and her 
consulting firm terminate its existing contracts and subcontracts for the provision o(public­
outreach sen1ices on behalf o(Falcon Engineering? 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in the making, or 
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the 
official has a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that an official has a .. financial interest" in a 
decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have 
material financial effect on one or more of the official's interests identified in that section. Of the 
interests identified in Section 87103, the decisions at issue and the facts presented implicate the 
Board Member's source-of-income interests in Village Enterprises and Falcon Engineering.2 
Regulation 18701 ( a) provides that a decision's financial effect on an official's financial 
interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is a named party in, or the subject 
of, a decision before the official's agency. An interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision 
involves the issuance renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other 
entitlement to, or contract with, the interest. (Ibid.) 
Regulation 18702.3 sets forth the materiality standards applicable to a decision's effect on 
an official's source-of-income interest. And Regulation 18702.3(a)(l) provides that a decision's 
financial effect is material if the source of income is a claimant, applicant, respondent, contracting 
party, or is otherwise named or identified as the subject of the proceeding. 
If Falcon Engineering submitted a proposal in response to the SBCT A RFP at issue, it 
would be an applicant to provide the services described in the RFP. Therefore, the decisions relating 
to Falcon Engineering's potential proposal would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial 
effect on the Board Member's source-of-income interest in Falcon Engineering. Thus, the Act 
prohibits the Board Member from taking part in decisions relating to Falcon Engineering's proposal 
in response to the RFP. 
Because Section 87103(c) provides that an official has a source-of-income interest in any 
source of income aggregating $500 or more to the official within 12 months prior to the time when 
the decision is made, the Board Member will have a source-of-income interest in Falcon 
Engineering for 12 months following her final receipt of income from Falcon Engineering. 
2 Section 87103(c) identifies an official's interest in any source of income, except gifts or loans by a 
commercial lending institution made in the regular course of business on tenns available lo the public without regard to 
official status, aggregating $500 or more in value provided or promised to, or received by, the public official within 12 
months prior to the time when the decision is made. Section 82030(a) defines "income" for purposes of the Act, and 
provides in pertinent part that "(i]ncome of an individual also includes a pro rata share of any income of any business 
entity or trust in which the individual or spouse owns, directly, indirectly or beneficially, a JO-percent interest or 
greater." 


File No. A-17-118 
Page No. 6 
Therefore, the Act would prohibit the Board Member from taking part in decisions relating to the 
Falcon Engineering proposal in response to the RFP for 12 months following the Board Member's 
final receipt of income from Falcon Engineering. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
By: 
MFC:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Jack Woodside 
General Counsel 
Mc,ffiy 
Counsel, Legal Division

having any direct or indirect influence on the Board Member. 
3. The Act would prohibit the Board Member from taking part in decisions relating to the 
engineering services contract with Falcon Engineering for 12 months following the Board Member 
or her consulting firm's final receipt of income from Falcon Engineering.

J. Does Section 1090 prohibit the Board Member from making or participating in the 
maldng of, or the SBCTA Board o(Directors from entering into. the engineering services contract 
with Falcon Engineering?

We employ a six-step analysis to determine whether an official has a disqualifying conflict

employee; (2) does the matter involve a contract; (3) is the official making or participating in

noninterest exception apply; and, under appropriate facts, ( 6) does the rule of necessity apply? 
Steps one through four are not at issue here. A public officer is involved because the Board

necessity is not applicable based on the facts presented, as another engineering finn besides Falcon

Thus, we conclude that Section 1090 would prohibit the Board Member from making or

engineering services contract with Falcon Engineering i{the Board Member and her consulting

Would Section 1090 prohibit the SBCT A Board of Directors from entering into the engineering services contract with Falcon Engineering if the Board Member and her consufting finn terminate its existing contracts and subcontracts for the provision of public-outreach services on behalf of Falcon Engineering? 1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 8 I 000 through 91014. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No.A-17-118 Page No. 2 3. Would the Act prohibit the Board Member from taking part in governmental decisions relating to the engineering services contract with Falcon Engineering if the Board Member and her consulting firm terminate its existing contracts and subcontracts for the provision of public-outreach services on behalf of Falcon Engineering?
